growth_records.strain: strain 29 (DSM 17628) [name not validly published, quoted in source]

up.method: MIMS Vmax (accelerated from 11 to 72 over time)

growth_records.cite: Ida F. Peterse et al. Hydrogen Oxidation Benefits Alphaproteobacterial Methanotrophs Under Severe Methane Limitation. Environmental Microbiology (2025). DOI:10.1111/1462-2920.70163. [PMC12319191]
growth_records.snippet: these chemostats were maintained under stable dilution rates of D = 0.0057 h-1 for M. acidophilus 29... the H2 oxidation rates of M. acidophilus 29 during MIMS incubations accelerated over time... reaching rates comparable to those found fo
growth_records.notes: CH4/O2-limited chemostat D=0.0057 h-1 = mu; td=ln2/0.0057=121.6 h. Vmax CH4=75 umol/min/gDW (x0.06=4.50 mmol/gDW/h); Km(app) CH4=2.5-4 uM. Vmax H2 11->72 umol/min/gDW (0.66->4.32 mmol/gDW/h); Km(app) H2=0.04-0.07 uM. CAVEAT: chemostat culture contained a Burkholderia contaminant carrying a Group 3d [NiFe] hydrogenase (strain 29 ~4x more abundant); authors state H2 oxidation cannot be attributed solely to strain 29 (hence medium confidence for the H2 rate; CH4 Vmax is methanotroph-specific). No DW determined; authors used mean DW of other strains (250.3 mg DW/L). DO/temp/pH not explicitly stated for this reactor; reactor described as similar set-up to M. bryophila (5% air saturation, pH 6-6.3, 26-28C) so those values inferred.
